student_name: Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari
hub_subtitle: Inglês do dia a dia — apresentar-se, rotina e conversação

lesson.menu_title: Fluency Under Pressure -- Sustained Conversation and Spontaneous Production
lesson.menu_desc: The milestone lesson: keep a real conversation going without freezing. Everyday words for fluency like fluency, effortless, to stumble, to elaborate, pace, to keep up, tongue-tied, to get across, breakthrough and to sustain, plus the fluency toolkit that paraphrases a missing word, buys a second and keeps you talking -- 32 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 20 -- Fluency Under Pressure
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari | Lesson 20 -- Fluency Under Pressure

vocab.title: Match each fluency word to its meaning
defs: the ability to speak smoothly and easily
sure of yourself and not nervous
so easy that it needs no effort
to stop or make a mistake while speaking
to explain something in more detail
how fast or slow you speak
to say what you think or feel
to follow a fast conversation without falling behind
unable to speak because you are nervous
to make people understand your idea
to keep something going for a long time

paras: A year ago, every English conversation felt like hard work. I translated each sentence in my head before I spoke, so I was always one step behind. I would stumble on a word, freeze, and go tongue-tied. Small talk felt like a test I could fail, and I often stayed quiet just to be safe.

Then something changed. I stopped waiting for the perfect sentence. When I forgot a word, I did not panic -- I described it in other words and kept going. I learned to speak at a calm, steady pace and to use short connectors to sustain the conversation, even when I was not sure. Little by little, speaking became almost effortless.

Last week I talked with a colleague for ten minutes without translating once. I made mistakes, of course, but I got my point across, and I could keep up with everything she said. That was my real breakthrough: not speaking perfectly, but speaking freely. Fluency is not about zero mistakes. It is the confidence to express your ideas, keep the conversation going, and actually enjoy it.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B1 reading, a learner reflects on the moment English conversation became fluent and spontaneous

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: It explains the grammar rules you must memorize to pass an English exam.
Ana Paula became fluent not by speaking perfectly, but by keeping the conversation going, describing words she forgot, and expressing her ideas with confidence.
It says you should always translate every sentence in your head before you speak.

items: A year ago, Ana Paula translated sentences in her head before speaking.
The text says: 'I translated each sentence in my head before I spoke.'
When she forgot a word, she stopped the conversation completely.
The text says: 'I did not panic -- I described it in other words and kept going.'
Ana Paula talked with a colleague for ten minutes without translating.
The text says: 'I talked with a colleague for ten minutes without translating once.'
For Ana Paula, fluency means speaking with zero mistakes.
The text says: 'Fluency is not about zero mistakes.'

, and when I forget a word I
my idea in a different way. I can

gapfill.text: Spontaneous production means speaking without a script. The secret is simple: do not stop to translate. When a word is missing, paraphrase it -- say 'the thing you use to...' and keep going. Buy a second with 'let me think', speak at a calm pace, and pass the turn back with 'what about you?'. Fluency is not zero mistakes; it is keeping the conversation alive and getting your idea across.

bank.label: Phrases that keep a conversation going
items: Sorry, I forgot the word -- it is the thing you use to...
Let me put that another way.
That is a good question -- give me a second.
Can you tell me more about that?
I am not sure how to say this, but...

lf.title: What is each phrase doing?
items: When I forget a word, I say
To pass the turn back, I ask
To buy a second, I say

lf.text: Notice the four moves of a fluent speaker: paraphrase a missing word (what I mean is...), buy a little time (give me a second), pass the turn back (what about you?), and add detail (for example...). None of them need perfect grammar -- they just keep you talking and keep the conversation flowing.

items.situation: You forget the English word for something. Keep going without stopping.
tips: Sorry, I forgot the word -- it is the thing you use to cook.
What I mean is, the small kitchen tool.

items.situation: A friend asks about your weekend. Give a short answer, then pass the turn back.
tips: It was great -- I relaxed a lot. What about you?
Pretty busy! And how was yours?

items.situation: Someone asks a hard question and you need a second. Stay fluent, do not freeze.
tips: That is a good question -- give me a second.
Hmm, let me think... I would say...

items.situation: You want to add more detail to your idea. Elaborate.
tips: For example, last week I tried it and it worked.
What I mean is, it helps me because it saves time.

items.situation: You made a small mistake in the middle of a sentence. Keep going anyway.
tips: ...sorry, I mean yesterday -- anyway, as I was saying...
Let me put that another way.

items: I will ask you three simple questions. Answer each one and do not stop, even if you forget a word -- describe it in other words and carry on.
I will tell you something small about my week. React to it, then ask me a question to keep the conversation going.
Pick any topic you like -- your work, your city, a plan. Talk to me about it for two minutes without translating in your head.
We chat freely. If you forget a word or make a mistake, do not stop -- paraphrase, buy a second, and keep the conversation flowing.

answerkey.title: Reveal the fluency toolkit
list: Forgot a word? Paraphrase: It is the thing you use to... / What I mean is...
Need a second? Buy time: Give me a moment. / Let me think.
Keep it going: Pass the turn -- What about you? / Tell me more.
Add detail: Elaborate -- For example... / because...
Made a mistake? Keep going: Sorry, I mean... anyway, as I was saying...
answerkey.note: Fluency is not zero mistakes -- it is keeping the conversation alive and getting your idea across. You did it, Ana Paula.

listenings.text: Here is the secret that took me years to learn: fluency is not about knowing every word. It is about what you do when you do not know one. A fluent speaker does not freeze. If a word is missing, they describe it -- they say the thing you use for cooking, or the place where you buy bread -- and they keep going. They speak at a calm, steady pace, they are not afraid of small mistakes, and they always keep the conversation moving. So the next time a word disappears from your head, do not stop and translate. Paraphrase, breathe, and carry on. That is how you sustain a real conversation, and that is what real fluency sounds like.

Hi Ana Paula, it is Sarah! I just had to call and say congratulations. Do you remember our very first classes, when you translated every single sentence in your head before you spoke? Look at you now -- you finished the whole program, and last week you talked to your colleague for ten minutes without stopping once. I know you still make little mistakes, but honestly, so do I, and so does everyone. The point is, you keep the conversation going, you get your ideas across, and you actually enjoy speaking now. That is the real breakthrough. I am so proud of you. Let us celebrate soon. Call me back when you can. Bye!

extra_audio.text: A year ago, I translated every sentence in my head. I stumbled on hard words and often felt tongue-tied. Then I stopped waiting for the perfect sentence. When I forgot a word, I described it and kept going. Last week I spoke for ten minutes without translating, and that was my breakthrough.
